▪ Mean-variance (MV) portfolio problem week ▪ Setting up the problem and understanding the key inputs

▪ Forecasting expected returns and volatilities ▪ Predicting return on equities and bonds ▪ Predicting volatilities ▪ Long-term Capital Market Assumptions (LTCMAs) ▪ Using third party LTCMA forecasts

Week ▪ Solving the mean-variance (MV) portfolio problem for a multi-asset allocation ▪ Finding the optimal asset mix (optimal weights) ▪ Studying optimal portfolio performance

FINS 3640  Portfolio Management • https://www.blackrock.com/au/intermediaries/products/254711/blackrock-blk-global-allocation-fund-austd-fund

• « Portfolio management consists in investing your assets in a way that seeks to grow your financial wealth over time, or to reach specific financial objectives to carry out future projects. »

• « Since it can be done over different time horizons and must take into account your cash flow needs and the financial impact of your decisions, it requires expertise, experience and objectivity. »

• « You already know that your investments must be diversified – by sector, asset classes, country and management styles – and rebalanced over time to reduce the risk associated with market fluctuations. Yet, they must also keep in line with any change that may take place in your life. »

• « Therefore, the management approach you choose must seek capital accumulation while minimizing the volatility of your portfolio. »

FINS 3640 3  Steps in the PF Management Process • Purpose of this process: to achieve the return objective (E(R)) while considering the risk tolerance (individual or institutional) and respecting the constraints of the investors

• The main steps in the portfolio management process 1. Planning • Identification of customer needs • Definition of an investment policy: investment objectives, constraints 2. Execution • Determination of asset allocation • Choice of strategies (active, passive) • Construction of the PF 3. Feedback • Monitoring / rebalancing of the PF • Performance evaluation / reports

See Appendix A for a more detailed description of each step.

T.FILICHI rlnomption.lgForLet investment horizon with of W So with Shorter horizons Strategies based QFitat.ie Quantitativetrading strategies tradi on signals Ecr Ocr strategies models and Use of predictive based on rebalance strategy trade horizon forecast shorter  Investment Policy Statement (IPS) • An investment policy statement (IPS) is a document drafted between a portfolio manager and a client that outlines general rules for the manager.

• Specific information on matters such as asset allocation, risk tolerance and liquidity requirements are included in an investment policy statement.

• Objectives of the IPS: realistic choices and objectives taking into account the different constraints of the investor • Promotes a long-term management discipline: avoid panic or overconfidence • Allows better evaluation of the portfolio manager's performance (in particular by comparing it with a benchmark) • Protects investors from inappropriate asset management or unethical behavior on the part of managers

FINS 3640 6  Investment Policy Statement Investment objectives • Performance objective • Tolerance / risk aversion Constraints • Liquidity constraints • Investment horizon • Regulatory constraints • Tax considerations • Specific considerations FINS 3640 7  The Standard MV Portfolio Problem Steffen • The standard assumption is that investors like higher mean (expected returns) and dislike higher variance.

• Under this assumption the class of potentially optimal portfolios for such investors are therefore those with the greatest expected return for a given level of variance and, reciprocally, the smallest variance for a given expected return.

• Such portfolios are termed mean-variance efficient.

• This is the basic principle of portfolio construction of Markowitz (1952).

• This principle defines the industry practices of numerous pension funds and mutual funds.

• Today, we are doing a deep dive into the required steps for implementing an asset mix, that is, finding the optimal weights of a portfolio constituted of various financial assets.

FINS 3640 8   The Problem • We first need to find the minimum-variance portfolios in the absence of a riskless asset. • The minimum-variance portfolio with expected return μ𝑝 , is the solution 𝑤𝑖 for all asset i to:

• Forecasts for expected returns and volatility are at the really core of portfolio construction and the design of investment strategies including • Volatility-managed portfolios, • Long-short portfolio based on expected return forecasts, • etc…

• We now discuss the modeling and forecasting of returns and volatility.

asset nix are Asset allocation to market sensitive capital assumptions   Forecasting expected returns and volatilities • The quality of the forecasts critically depend on the horizon. • For expected returns, it is usually easier to forecast them at longer horizons than at shorter ones. • Easier to forecast next year returns, or next 5 years returns than next month/week/day returns. • For volatility, it is the opposite. Forecasting at shorter horizon is usually easier because volatility is persistent over time (i.e., low (high) volatility today is likely to be followed by low (high) volatility tomorrow).

• There are many ways to forecast return and volatility: • Aggregating market participants expectations about return and volatility. • Analyzing macro-level data (GDP growth, inflation, employment, immigration, etc…). • Use of models which require to estimate parameters (in-sample v.s. out-of-sample model performance). • Different models work well for different forecast horizons.

• Realized/historical v.s. expected returns and volatility • Knowing the past does not mean knowing the future (in-sample v.s. out-of-sample model performance) • Changes in regulations, unexpected macro-events (war, pandemic, country defaulting on sovereign debts, etc…) are not always foreseeable.

FINS 3640 11  Forecasting using OLS • Various methodology can be used to forecast returns or volatilities. One approach is to adopt predictive OLS regressions.

• The idea of predictive regression is relatively simple. The goal is to predict future return/volatility using variables informative about expected returns/volatility.

• The variable to be predicted corresponds to the return or volatility next period: next month return/volatility, next year return/volatility, …

• The explanatory variables used to predict future return/volatility is lagged: They correspond to the variables that would be observed or that could be constructed one period before the return/volatility to be predicted. • If a variable helps predict future return/volatility (i.e., is statistically significant), we say that this variable has pred ictive power. • If a variable has predictive power, then, an investor could use it to predict return/volatility (i.e., obtain an estimate of expected return/volatility) and trade accordingly on the signal obtained.

• Predictive regression take the form: it 𝑦𝑡 = α + β 𝑥𝑡−1 + u𝑡

where 𝑦𝑡 is period-t return or volatility and 𝑥𝑡−1 is the predictor(s) computed one period before.

• If our model performs better out-of-sample than the benchmark, then, σ𝑡 𝑒𝑟𝑟𝑡𝐵 2 > σ𝑡 𝑒𝑟𝑟𝑡 2 and 𝑅𝑂𝑂𝑆 > 0. • If our model performs worse out-of-sample than the benchmark, then, σ𝑡 𝑒𝑟𝑟𝑡𝐵 2 < σ𝑡 𝑒𝑟𝑟𝑡 2 and 𝑅𝑂𝑂𝑆 < 0.

be 20 Outof Sample R squared can  Python Application 1: Predicting SPX returns • Jan Martin, a famous finance academics from LSE, has shown that the VIX-squared is a good proxy for stock market expected returns. • The VIX is a volatility index constructed from derivatives. This index is available in real-time just like the S&P 500.

Python Application: • Step 1 - We download SPX and VIX data for the last 20 years. • Step 2 - We calculate 1-month future SPX returns and create a DataFrame combining VIX at time t and future SPX returns for the next month recorded as of time t. • Step 3 - Using a 10-year fixed rolling window, we estimate the regression model with VIX-squared predicting future SPX returns: 𝑟𝑆𝑃,𝑡 = α + β 𝑉𝐼𝑋𝑡−1 + u𝑡 . We construct each month the model forecast for next month S&P 500 and stored out-of-sample predictions. • Step 4 - Out-of-Sample R-squared: We compute the out-of-sample R-squared based on out-of-sample predictions of the model and compared them to a 5-year rolling mean S&P500 return benchmark model.

• See Application 1 - Predicting SPX returns.ipynb for the solution.

Forecasting using OLS In- and out-of-sample performance: • The more explanatory variables a model has, the better its explanatory power in-sample in general. • But a good in-sample performance does not always translate to good out-of-sample performance. • The predictive power of models is thus generally assessed out-of-sample (i.e., outside of the time-period used to estimate it). • Just like the way an investor would implement a model to trade on it: estimate the model using data available up to the tradi ng date and then use the model predictions to trade on it out-of-sample. Choice of explanatory variables: • Finding variables 𝑥𝑡 that robustly predict returns is notoriously hard. • VIX (Volatility index of SPX); Earning price ratio (E/P); dividend payout ratio (D/E); Dividend-price ratio (D/P); net equity expansion (new equity issued/aggregate market capitalization); percent equity issuing (ratio of equity issue to total equity and debt issuance); Book-to-market ratio (B/M); Term spread (TMS) - Difference between the long-term government yield and the Treasury bill rate; Default yield spread (DFY) - Difference

inflation between BAA-and AAA-rated corporate bond yields; etc … • Finding variables 𝑥𝑡 that robustly predict corporate bond returns is also hard, but bond returns are easier to predict than stock returns. • On top of stock return predictors: Aggregate leverage ratio (LEV) – average ratio of book value of debt to the sum of book debt and market equity value; 5-year government bond yield; Stock index returns; proportion of junk bond issuers to total bond issuers (IQ); pro portion of longterm debt issued relative to total debt issued

• Finding variables 𝑥𝑡 that robustly predict volatility is much easier than for predicting returns • Lagged measures of variance: last week realized variance, last month realized variance, last quarter realized variance, last year realized variance.

FINS 3640 21  Forecasting equity returns β Goyal and Welch (2008): • Use an extensive list of predictors for market equity returns. youth later • Estimate predictive regressions 𝑟𝑡 = α+ β 𝑥𝑡−1 + u𝑡 where 𝑥𝑡−1 is a given predictor and 𝑟𝑡 is month-t market return. • Use an out-of-sample expanding rolling window approach. • Their analysis is based on monthly returns. It starts on December 1927 and ends in December 2004. • To construct their first forecast, they estimate the coefficients using an initial estimation of 20 years of monthly data (from December 1927 to December 1947). Estimate using data from December 1927 to December 1947, estimate α𝐷𝑒𝑐 1947 and β𝐷𝑒𝑐 1947 Forecast for market return for January 1948: α𝐷𝑒𝑐 1947 + β𝐷𝑒𝑐 1947 𝑥𝐷𝑒𝑐 1947 Forecast error in January 1948: 𝑒𝑟𝑟𝐽𝑎𝑛 1948 = 𝑟𝐽𝑎𝑛 1948 - (α𝐷𝑒𝑐 1947 + β𝐷𝑒𝑐 1947 𝑥𝐷𝑒𝑐 1947 )

• Then, they recursively re-estimate the coefficients by adding the new (monthly) observation. For instance, Estimate using data from December 1927 to January 1948, estimate α𝐽𝑎𝑛 1948 and β𝐽𝑎𝑛 1948 Forecast for market return for February 1948: α𝐽𝑎𝑛 1948 + β𝐽𝑎𝑛 1948 𝑥𝐽𝑎𝑛 1948 Forecast error in February 1948: : 𝑒𝑟𝑟𝐽𝑎𝑛 1948 = 𝑟𝐹𝑒𝑏 1948 - (α𝐽𝑎𝑛 1948 + β𝐽𝑎𝑛 1948 𝑥𝐽𝑎𝑛 1948 )

• And the algorithm continues….  Forecasting equity returns Goyal and Welch (2008): • Study in-sample (IS) and out-of-sample (OOS) R-squared. • The definition of R-squared here is different from that of the OLS regression but has a similar interpretation. A higher Rsquared means a better predictive power of a given variable. • *,**,*** next to R-squared indicate the statistical significance (more * is good and none means that the R-squared is not significant from 0 predictability). In-sample R-squared Out-of-sample R-squared u

1 1 1 1 • In-sample explanatory power of predictive regression higher than out-of-sample. • While some variables help predict BB market equity return in-sample, only 3 variables have statistically significant predictive power out-ofsample (percent equity issuing, one term-spread, and inflation). • Predicting equity return is hard!

Forecasting bond returns E t.EE ttitt Lin, Wu, and Zhou (2015): Averageof predictions • Use an extensive list of stock and bond predictors to forecast bond returns by credit Et il re rating. Estimate predictive regressions 𝑟𝑡 = α+ β 𝑥𝑡−1 + u𝑡 where 𝑥𝑡−1 is a given predictor and 𝑟𝑡 is month-t bond return. Then they use the α and β obtained to construct model forecast out-of-sample for each variable and use the average of all forecasts as a single predictor. • Study in-sample and out-of-sample R-squared:

In-sample R-squared Out-of-sample R-squared • The performance of the model that combines all predictor forecast is somewhat good. • By comparison, the bond portfolio returns are easier to forecast than the equity market index. • Potential reasons: more institutional investors in bonds which makes bond return less noisy and more predictable. • Predicting bond returns is easier than equity!

FINS 3640 24   Forecasting volatilities: HAR model • Corsi (2009) proposes an OLS predictive model for variance: HAR. The model uses lagged variance measures to predict future variance. 𝑑 • The original model uses next day realized variance as the variable to be predicted (𝑅𝑉𝑡+1 ). • As predictor, the model uses (lagged) one-day realized variance (𝑅𝑉𝑡𝑑 ), weekly realized variance (𝑅𝑉𝑡𝑤 ) and monthly realized variance (𝑅𝑉𝑡𝑚 ). All these measures are computed using up time t information. week 𝑑 daily pay𝑑 𝑑 𝑤 𝑤 • The model is as follows: 𝑅𝑉 = α + β 𝑅𝑉 + β 𝑅𝑉 + β 𝑅𝑉 + u 𝑡+1 𝑡 𝑡 𝑚 𝑡 𝑚 menthol

• Suppose that we have estimated the model parameters using information up to time t.

• Then, the expected volatility from period t+1 to t+K (i.e., volatility for the next K periods) based on period t information is given by:

• If we estimate the model using daily returns and set K=21, then 𝜎𝑡+1:𝑡+21 is the forecast of next month variance on day t.

• If we estimate the model using monthly returns and set K=12, then 𝜎𝑡+1:𝑡+12 is the forecast of next year variance on month t.

0.05464 weekly variance GH Ets 52 0.02841 annualized variance  Long-term Capital Market Assumptions (LTCMAs) for Stock and Bond Returns For portfolio construction, we are usually interested with long-term trends: • Numerous applications in finance require constructing a portfolio for long-term investors (superannuation and retirement funds, etc…). • Long-horizon forecast focuses on central tendencies (long-term fair value) rather than transitory price discrepancies. • The challenge is thus to find the long-term fair value of the components of expected returns.

Components of equity returns: • Total return on equity is the sum of capital gains and income yield. For stocks, income yield is simply the dividend yield. We have: 𝑃 𝐷 1 + 𝑟𝑡+1 = 𝑡+1 + 𝑃𝑡+1 𝑃𝑡 𝑡

• We can show (see Appendix B) that the return on equity is the sum of the dividend yield ( 𝐷𝑌𝑡+1 ), the growth in 𝑃𝑡+1 𝐸 𝑃𝐸 𝐸 price-earning/price-dividend ratio (𝐺𝑀𝑡+1 = 𝑃𝑡+1 𝑡 = 𝑃𝐸𝑡+1), and earning/dividend growth rate (𝐺𝐸𝑡+1 = 𝑡+1 ). 𝑡 𝐸𝑡 𝐸𝑡

𝑟𝑡+1 = 𝐷𝑌𝑡+1 + 𝐺𝑀𝑡+1 + G𝐸𝑡+1 and thus 𝐸𝑡 [𝑟𝑡+1 ] = 𝐸𝑡 [𝐷𝑌𝑡+1 ]+ 𝐸𝑡 [𝐺𝑀𝑡+1 ] + 𝐸𝑡 [G𝐸𝑡+1 ] • Each components needs to be forecasted over the desire horizon (i.e., 10 years).

FINS 3640 34  Long-term Capital Market Assumptions (LTCMAs) for Stock and Bond Returns Components of corporate bond returns: • Total return on bond is the sum of income yield, capital gains, and credit losses. We have: 𝐶 𝑃𝑡+1 −𝑃𝑡 𝐶𝐿𝑡+1 𝑟𝑡+1 = 𝑡+1 + - 𝑃𝑡 𝑃𝑡 𝑃𝑡 𝑃𝑡+1 −𝑃𝑡 𝐶 • Using duration return approximation for 𝑃𝑡 = −𝐷𝑡 (𝑦𝑡+1- 𝑦𝑡 ) and assuming the bond is at par ( 𝑡+1 is equal to 𝑃𝑡 YTM 𝑦𝑡 ) 𝐶𝐿𝑡+1 𝐸𝑡 [𝑟𝑡+1 ] = 𝑦𝑡 −𝐷𝑡 𝐸𝑡 [ 𝑦𝑡+1 - 𝑦𝑡 ] - 𝐸𝑡 [ ] 𝑃𝑡

• Each components needs to be forecasted over the desire horizon (i.e., 10 years).

What about other asset classes such as illiquid assets? • Private equity, private debt, infrastructure, real estate return forecasts can also be done. • Each asset class requires its own modeling assumptions. • For instance, infrastructure transactions are usually highly levered, and the effect of interest rate and inflation will be taken into account for return forecast.

• Based on these assets, we will estimate an optimal asset mix. Our LTCMAs for expected returns will be the sample average and the LTCMAs for variances and covariances will be the sample estimates.

• We will target a portfolio return of 9% annualized and add constraints on maximum and minimum weights.

FINS 3640 41   Portfolio performance analysis • Various measures of performance are usually reported when considering investment strategies and portfolio allocations.

• Often, a benchmark is used to analyze the relative performance of the investment strategy considered. Ideally, the benchmark chosen should be relevant and comparable in terms of risk profile to the strategy analyzed. • Market portfolio, balanced portfolio (60% in equity and 40% in bonds), large diversified portfolio of equities, aggregate portfolio of the NAV of other managers, etc…

potential Benchmarks • In addition, various statistical measures are reported. The goal of these measures is to complement the expected return and volatility estimates of the portfolio. • Sharpe ratio. • Probability of negative/positive returns. • Maximum drawdown: is a measure of an asset's largest price drop from a peak to a trough. Maximum drawdown is considered to be an indicator of downside risk. A large maximum drawdown is indicative of the intensity of crashes. • Value at risk (VaR) 95%: is a measure of the risk of loss for investments. It estimates how an investment might lose with a given probability. For instance, the VaR 95% of the returns of an investment tell us the negative returns that could happen with 5% probability. When using historical returns, it corresponds to the percentile 5% of historical returns, that is, the historical return which has 5% of returns equal or below it. • Expected Shortfall (ES) 95%: also known as Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR), is a risk measure that provides an estimate of the average loss that occurs beyond a specified VaR threshold. Specifically, the Expected Shortfall at the 95% level (ES 95%) refers to the average loss in the worst 5% of cases. ES 95% represents the average loss that would occur in scenarios where losses exceed the VaR 95%. It provides insight into the tail risk of a portfolio.

FINS 3640 43   Appendix A: PF Management Process 1. Planning Phase: Identification of Customer Needs • This initial stage focuses on understanding the client’s financial situation, goals, risk tolerance, time horizon, liquidity needs, and any other factors that influence the investment strategy. A comprehensive assessment of the client's financial objectives (retirement, wealth preservation, etc.) is essential to tailor the portfolio accordingly. Definition of an Investment Policy: Investment Objectives and Constraints • The investment policy outlines specific goals (e.g., capital appreciation, income generation) and constraints (e.g., risk tolerance, liquidity, time horizon, legal or regulatory restrictions, tax considerations). The policy serves as the framework for decision-making throughout the portfolio management process. Investment Objectives: • Define the purpose of the investments—whether to achieve long-term growth, generate income, or preserve wealth. Constraints: • Address any limitations or requirements that might affect portfolio construction, including the need for liquidity, legal regulations, tax concerns, or any unique client circumstances (ethical preferences, geographical restrictions). • The investment policy should be a formal document that clearly states these objectives and constraints.

FINS 3640 44   Appendix A: PF Management Process 2. Execution Phase Determination of Asset Allocation • Asset allocation is the most critical step in portfolio construction. It involves deciding how much of the portfolio should be invested in different asset classes (stocks, bonds, real estate, commodities, etc.) based on the client’s risk tolerance and investment objectives. This step ensures dive rsification and balances risk and return. Strategic Asset Allocation: • A long-term approach that sets target allocations to different asset classes based on the investor’s risk profile and time horizon. Tactical Asset Allocation: • A more dynamic approach that adjusts the portfolio in the short term based on market conditions or opportunities. Choice of Strategies (Active vs. Passive) • Once asset allocation is determined, the next step is to select investment strategies: Active Strategy: • Actively managed portfolios aim to outperform the market through stock selection, market timing, or other forms of active man agement. Passive Strategy: • Passive strategies aim to replicate a market index (such as the S&P 500), focusing on minimizing costs and maintaining a long -term perspective. • A combination of both active and passive strategies might be employed, depending on the client’s goals and the efficiency of the markets being targeted. Construction of the Portfolio • This step involves selecting specific investments (stocks, bonds, funds) within the predetermined asset allocation. The portfolio manager must ensure that the investments align with the strategy and that they are diversified enough to mitigate risk while still meeting the expected return.

FINS 3640 45   Appendix A: PF Management Process 3. Feedback Phase Monitoring / Rebalancing of the Portfolio • Portfolio monitoring is an ongoing process that ensures the portfolio remains aligned with the client’s goals and investment policy. This involves: Rebalancing: • Periodically adjusting the portfolio to maintain the desired asset allocation. This may involve selling over-performing assets and buying under-performing ones to keep the portfolio on track. Monitoring Risks and Returns: • Regularly reviewing the portfolio’s performance to ensure it meets the expected returns while managing risk levels. Performance Evaluation / Reports • Performance evaluation involves measuring how the portfolio has performed relative to benchmarks or specific objectives. This is typically done through: Absolute Performance: • Evaluating the portfolio’s overall return. Relative Performance: • Comparing the portfolio’s performance to a relevant benchmark or peer group. Risk-Adjusted Performance: • Metrics like the Sharpe Ratio, Treynor Ratio, or Alpha are used to assess performance in the context of the portfolio's risk level. • Performance reports should be regularly provided to clients, summarizing the portfolio’s performance, any major changes, and the outlook for future periods.

FINS 3640 46  Appendix B: Equity return components • Equation 7 is the standard equation of total return: sum of capital gains (CG) + dividend yield (DY). 𝐸𝑡+1 𝐸𝑡+1 • Then CG can be multiplied by 𝐸𝑡 which is equal to 𝐸𝑡 1. • After some rewriting you get equation 8. • Since the product 𝐺𝑀𝑡+1 G𝐸𝑡+1 is small, you can assume it is 0. Doing so, and expanding equation 8 you get: • 𝐶𝐺𝑡+1 = 𝐺𝑀𝑡+1+ G𝐸𝑡+1 • Plugging this result into 7 you get • 𝑅𝑡+1= 𝐺𝑀𝑡+1+ G𝐸𝑡+1 + 𝐷𝑌𝑡+1
